notes: Sharp reflex expletive. Most instinctive single-word curse.
Trails off; she rarely finishes it. Frustration, not rage.
Shock/pain reaction more than anger. Also trails off.
Direct dismissal. Lit. something like 'save your life elsewhere.'

english: Oh, this is bad / bad winds coming
notes: Dread, not explosive. Said quietly when things are going sideways.
Directly parallel to 'God give me strength.' The nuns' version.

english: Pyre and ash / we're done for
notes: Paired noun curse. The Asmodean equivalent of 'fire and brimstone.' Used when something is irreversibly bad.

english: I'm not calling on His name
notes: Said carefully when she nearly invokes Asmodeus by reflex. Old nun-taught caution.
Genuine prayer-reflex. Not ironic. The closest she gets to actually praying.
Single word. Cold and formal. Used to dismiss something or someone — or a thought.
Most instinctive single-word expletive for pure shock. Contracted.
Self-directed. Said when she makes a mistake she should have seen coming.

english: We're cursed / the devil's got our number
notes: Fatalistic. Said when things keep going wrong in a pattern.
Total frustration with a situation. Lit. 'into the fire, all of it.'

english: Shut up shut up shut up
notes: Repeated for emphasis. Can be directed outward or at her own thoughts.
Formally polite on the surface. Said when things turn deadly serious — a warning dressed as a farewell.
Dangerous agreement. When she says this coldly, it means she's accepted something she didn't want to.
Full stop. Used to end arguments, threats, or her own spiraling thoughts.

english: Do what you want / Have it your way
notes: Dismissive or threatening depending on context. She's done arguing.
Invokes Asmodeus's omniscience. Said when something can't be taken back — a reminder, maybe a threat.
COINED. Liturgical structure, strong paired curse. From sanguis et damnis. Said mid-frustration with a heavy subject. First appeared: Rova 6, 4725 AR.
COINED. Contracted from callidus acutus, 'sharp clever one.' Used as a directed insult. First target: Leofric. First appeared: Rova 6, 4725 AR.
COINED. Adapted from In ignem omnia — same structure, targeted (eum = him). First appeared: Rova 6, 4725 AR.
COINED. Contracted from anima miseranda, 'soul to be pitied.' The nuns said prayers like this over the fallen. Caelia uses it with genuine sadness. First appeared: Rova 7, 4725 AR.
Not coined — a foundational word. Asmodean doctrine runs on contracts; she likely learned this before she learned the Common word for it. Slips out naturally.
notes2: First appeared in writing: Rova 7, 4725 AR.

notes: COINED. Contracted from quid in inferis, 'what in hell?!' Bewildered expletive, not angry — pure shock. First appeared: Rova 7, 4725 AR (without further elaboration).
